author: jpata
body:
#### PR description:

Updates the ML-based particle flow (MLPF) inference code and model file to the state used for [PFT-25-001](https://cms.cern.ch/iCMS/analysisadmin/cadilines?line=PFT-25-001&tp=an&id=2958&ancode=PFT-25-001) and as submitted to the journal in [2601.17554v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.17554v1). This PR does not intend to propose a production-ready and final model. The goal is to enable validation, cross-checks and further development by the rest of the collaboration. **MLPF is NOT used in any production worfklow, and no production workflows should be affected.**

Summary of the changes:
- The model onnx file switches from locality sensitive hashing based graph neural network (GNN-LSH) to a more standard transformer-based architecture, with around ~5M parameters for the transformer
- The transformer uses a fused flash attention operation on CUDA directly via ONNXRuntime, and has been tested on L4 GPUs via CERN OpenLab. Relevant plots are in the [AN2024_253_v12](http://cms.cern.ch/iCMS/jsp/openfile.jsp?tp=draft&files=AN2024_253_v12.pdf), Fig 19-20.
- Anticipating the CPU fallback questions:
  - it runs on CPU out of the box using naive matrix ops, which are somewhat more memory and CPU intensive than the previous custom architecture. Nevertheless, the transformer-based model is preferred due to resulting in significantly improved physics reconstruction fidelity, and fast runtime and low memory usage on GPUs from the Ampere generation onwards.
  - While cross-checking the inference is possible on CPU, this model is not intended to be used today on CPU in a production setting. Work is ongoing to explore CPU-friendly architectures with comparable performance for a future version.
- The model output is deterministic, and generally returns compatible outputs between different inference devices in our tests.
- On the MLPFProducer.cc, we update the feature set, and ensure the model outputs are interpreted to PFCandidates while preserving the links to the inputs.
- The MLPFProducer uses standard ONNXRuntime local inference, but overall the approach is agnostic to the inference backend. Nothing in the scheme specifically requires local inference, or prevents other kinds of inference schemes to be tested. I believe this would make more sense as central effort led by the ML prod team, rather than doing it case-by-base by model developers.
- On the generator side, we add the configuration snippets to generate the training datasets.
- On the matrix workflow configuration side, we update the MLPF test workflows (17034.13, 17043.13) to use 2025PU configuration, as the 2022PU files are no longer available.

The corresponding data PR is here: https://github.com/cms-data/RecoParticleFlow-PFProducer/pull/5

#### PR validation:
The validations are linked in CADI under pre-approval, approval and the AN: [PFT-25-001](https://cms.cern.ch/iCMS/analysisadmin/cadilines?line=PFT-25-001&tp=an&id=2958&ancode=PFT-25-001)

Generally, to test locally, one should use:
```
runTheMatrix.py -l 17034.13 --what upgrade,standard,highstats,pileup,generator,extendedgen,production,ged,machine,premix
runTheMatrix.py -l 17043.13 --what upgrade,standard,highstats,pileup,generator,extendedgen,production,ged,machine,premix
```

#### Inputs needed:
A specific point that will benefit from expert input (@cms-sw/ml-l2 @cms-sw/heterogeneous-l2) and should probably be changed is the current manual definition of
```
desc.add<bool>("use_cuda", false);
```
that switches the ONNXRuntime from CPU to CUDA.
I'd be happy to change this to the best practice suggested by the experts, but I was not able to find prior examples.

FYI also @cms-sw/pf-l2 @cms-sw/reconstruction-l2
title: MLPF Run 3 inference code and model update
